article_title: Norway's Largest Viking Age Coin Hoard Surfaces at Mørstad Farm, With More Than 3,000 Silver Pieces and Counting
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Norway's Largest Viking Age Coin Hoard Surfaces at Mørstad Farm, With More Than 3,000 Silver Pieces and Counting
content_preview.summary: Two metal detectorists found 19 silver coins in a field near Rena on April 10, 2026. Within days, archaeologists had recovered more than 3,000, surpassing a record that had stood since 1836.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Metal detectorists Rune Sætre and Vegard Sørlie were searching a field in eastern Norway on April 10 when their devices began turning up silver. They retrieved 19 coins before stopping and alerting archaeologists, [according to Archaeology Magazine](https://archaeology.org/news/2026/04/30/viking-coin-hoard-discovered-in-norway/). Within roughly a week, professionals working alongside the pair had recovered more than 3,000 silver coins from the same plot, [as Scientific American repo...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: High-quality 719-word News piece on the Mørstad hoard. Clear structure (Overview / What We Know / Who Is Involved / What We Don't Know / Context). Tone is professional, neutral, and appropriately bounded — flags evolving coin counts (2,970 vs 3,000+ vs 3,150) explicitly rather than picking one figure.
editor_notes.source_verification:
All 6 source snapshots read; every claim verified verbatim.

• source-0.html.gz (Scientific American, May 1 2026): Confirms Rena, Østerdalen, Innlandet County; April 10 + 19 coins discovery; 980s-1040s C.E. dating; coins originating from England, Germany, Denmark, Norway; verbatim Smiseth quote 'a truly unique discovery of the kind one might only experience once in an entire career'; verbatim Andreas Bjelland Eriksen quote 'This is a historic find. The fact that it is also from the Viking Age makes it even more spectacular' (correctly identified as Minister of Climate and Environment); last comparable find 1950.

• source-1.html.gz (Smithsonian Magazine, May 4): Confirms Arstad hoard reference, 1,850 coins, 1836 discovery, 'nearly 200 years' record framing; 2,970 figure; Jostein Bergstøl as archaeologist at Museum of Cultural History.

• source-2.html.gz (Archaeology Magazine, Apr 30): Confirms verbatim 'They have done a fantastic job and followed the regulations to the letter' Smiseth quote; verbatim 'almost look newly minted' Smiseth quote; 'leather pouch and scattered by plowing after the pouch decayed' detail (article paraphrases as 'leather pouch that decayed underground, leaving the coins to be scattered by centuries of plowing'); around 1050 burial date; verbatim Gullbekk 'So not only is the discovery itself extraordinary, but it's also connected to a very interesting period of Norwegian history'.

• source-3.html.gz (Science Norway): Confirms 'Mørstad' hoard name from the farm; verbatim Gullbekk 'extraordinary' and 'truly exceptional' descriptions; around 1050 dating.

• source-4.html.gz (Archaeology News / archaeologymag.com): Confirms 3,150 figure; coins linked to Cnut the Great, Æthelred II, Otto III, Harald Hardrada (verbatim list); Coin Cabinet at Museum of Cultural History continues identification work.

• source-5.html.gz (Arkeonews): Confirms Harald Hardrada + Byzantium + Norwegian national minting system around 1045 connection.

Every inline link points to a source containing the specific claim. No misattribution. No fabricated quantitative claims.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All factual claims verified verbatim against snapshots. The article responsibly handles competing coin counts across outlets (Smithsonian's 2,970 / 3,000+ / Archaeology News's 3,150) by noting the excavation is ongoing rather than choosing one number. All 5+ direct quotations appear verbatim in the cited snapshots.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism — the headline framing ('largest in history' is sources' framing, not the article's editorialising). No AI self-reference. Appropriate use of 'spectacular' / 'extraordinary' as quoted attributions rather than the writer's voice.
editor_notes.originality: Searched archive for 'Viking', 'Mørstad', 'Norway hoard', 'coin hoard' — no prior coverage. Topic is new.
recommendations: Future housekeeping: smithsonianmag.com is a particularly strong candidate for the allowlist (long-running, reputable). archaeology.org (Archaeological Institute of America), sciencenorway.no, and archaeologymag.com / arkeonews.net are reputable specialist outlets — consider on a case-by-case basis.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. High-quality archaeology story with 6 well-attributed sources, every claim verified verbatim, and responsible handling of evolving coin counts. Verdict driven solely by 5-source allowlist gap (only Scientific American is on the list). Article is publishable as-is.
